figures.caption: Figure 1. Hierarchical Incremental Attribution. When new generative models emerge, IncreFA rapidly adapts without forgetting previous ones by learning hierarchically orthogonal priors and replaying latent memories.

Table 1. Comparative Experiments with Other Incremental Learning Methods. Following EP1, in our experiments, we report the average accuracy for the current task, and upon completion of the final task, we report the unseen detection rate. †We designate the more recently released Nano-banana and Imagen3 as unseen models to evaluate the model’s detection capability for unseen instances. ‡We directly employ a frozen CLIP model with a linear layer for training.

Table 2. Comparison with Other Attribution Methods. The experimental setup employs EP2, with metrics comprising per-model attribution accuracy and final average accuracy. †We designate the more recently released Nano-banana and Imagen3 as unseen models to evaluate the model’s detection capability for unseen instances. ‡We directly employ a frozen CLIP model with a linear layer for training.

Table 5. Composition of IABench. We collected 28 categories of generative models along with real images sourced from the COCO dataset, and partitioned them into training and test sets.†We generate all images following there official github.

Figure 3. Pipeline of IncreFA. IncreFA consists of two components: Hierarchical Constraints and Latent Memory Bank. For tasks in the data stream, visual features are first extracted using a pretrained large model, from which samples are drawn to form the latent memory bank. Subsequently, the visual features are projected to obtain a feature space with a hierarchical structure, on which classification is performed. The latent memory bank not only handles replay for old tasks but also employs Random Mixing to simulate unseen samples, thereby enhancing the model’s detection capability for unseen instances.
